ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific grilled salmon curry rice entry; FOODON_00002809 (curry rice food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based dish with fish and sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated grilled salmon curry rice code; A0FJP (rice-based dish) is used as the closest defensible category anchor. Specific salmon curry rice sub-code not available in FoodEx2 v2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for grilled salmon curry rice as a composite dish; not clinically coded as a distinct food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of grilled salmon curry rice is approximately 600 g, comprising a grilled salmon fillet (~150 g), steamed white rice (~200 g), and Japanese curry sauce with vegetables (~250 g).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Composite estimation from salmon (grilled), white rice (cooked), and Japanese-style curry sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for grilled salmon curry rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central entries: cooked Atlantic salmon (FDC ID 175167), cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756), and a Japanese curry sauce analogue (FDC ID 172949, beef curry with vegetables used as proxy for sauce component). Approximate dish composition assumed: 25% salmon, 33% rice, 42% curry sauce with vegetables by weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for grilled salmon curry rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 65 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) blended with curry sauce and vegetables which moderate the overall glycaemic response. The composite dish GI is estimated at approximately 65 (medium category) reflecting the dilution of white rice GI by protein (7.5g/100g from salmon) and fat (6.7g/100g from curry sauce), consistent with published mixed-meal GI attenuation principles (Brand-Miller et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (79.8g from 600g × 13.3g/100g) / 100 = 51.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 250 mg/100g (1500 mg per 600g serving) approaches the 1500–2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request curry sauce on the side and limit sauce quantity to reduce sodium intake; the omega-3 fatty acids from grilled salmon provide a beneficial vasodilatory effect that partially offsets cardiovascular sodium risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice is the dominant carbohydrate source with a high GI (~72); the 600g serving delivers approximately 79.8g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~52, which is clinically significant for postprandial glucose management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should substitute brown rice or reduce the rice portion by at least half, retain the salmon portion for its protein and fat-mediated glycaemic buffering, and request reduced curry sauce to lower the overall carbohydrate and glycaemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Grilled salmon provides EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids which reduce triglycerides and support endothelial function; however, sodium at 1500 mg per serving and saturated fat at 10.2 mg per serving require monitoring.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease benefit from the omega-3 content of grilled salmon but should limit curry sauce quantity to control sodium and saturated fat intake, and prefer a smaller rice portion to reduce overall caloric and glycaemic load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Grilled salmon is a rich source of EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids which directly reduce synovial inflammation; saturated fat at 1.7g/100g is well below the 5g threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis are encouraged to consume this dish for its anti-inflammatory omega-3 content from salmon, while requesting reduced soy sauce and curry sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Salmon Curry Rice is a Japanese-style rice dish popular in Singapore's Japanese casual dining and food delivery market. The dish consists of a grilled Atlantic salmon fillet served alongside steamed white rice and a rich Japanese curry sauce made from curry roux, onions, carrots, and potatoes. The salmon is typically seasoned and grilled until the skin is crisp and the flesh is moist and flaky, delivering a savoury, slightly smoky flavour. The Japanese curry sauce is mild to medium in spice level, thick, and slightly sweet compared to Indian or Thai curries, with a characteristic umami depth from the roux. The steamed white rice absorbs the curry sauce and provides the primary carbohydrate base of the meal. Per 100g, the dish provides approximately 142 kcal, 7.5g protein, 6.7g fat, and 13.3g carbohydrate. The 600g standard plate serving delivers approximately 852 kcal and 79.8g carbohydrate, making it a high-glycaemic-load meal (GL ~52) that requires portion management for individuals with type 2 diabetes. The sodium content of 250 mg/100g (1500 mg per serving) is clinically significant for hypertensive patients. The omega-3 fatty acids from grilled salmon provide cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish or wheat allergies. It is commonly served as a lunch or dinner main course.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for grilled salmon curry rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a USDA FoodData Central weighted composite of grilled salmon, cooked white rice, and a curry sauce proxy. The GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI attenuated by protein and fat content per published mixed-meal principles. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach and the absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a USDA FoodData Central weighted composite (approx. 25% grilled salmon, 33% cooked white rice, 42% Japanese curry sauce with vegetables). No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available from approved composite estimate): vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Detailed micronutrient breakdown for this specific composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024 for the exact preparation. 3. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on white rice GI (~72, University of Sydney GI Database) attenuated by protein and fat content per Brand-Miller et al. mixed-meal principles; this is an analogous estimate, not a direct measurement. 4. Glycemic load per 600g serving = 65 × 79.8 / 100 = 51.9 (high GL). 5. Halal status not confirmed; Japanese curry roux may contain pork-derived ingredients. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8) are estimated from the salmon and curry oil components via USDA FoodData Central salmon and vegetable oil entries. 7. This dish is sold in Singapore under the menu code 554D; the record is classified as Japanese cuisine served in Singapore. Multilingual local names are provided for Chinese (烤三文鱼咖喱饭 / 烤三文魚咖喱飯), Malay (Nasi Kari Salmon Bakar), Tamil (வறுத்த சால்மன் கறி சோறு), and Hindi (ग्रिल्ड सैल्मन करी चावल) to support Singapore-market multilingual retrieval, in addition to the mandatory Japanese name (グリルサーモンカレーライス).
